title: Top 10 LED Panel Lights for False Ceilings in India (2026)
description: Top 10 LED panel lights for false ceilings in India (2026): Havells Nimbus, Octane and Trim NXT ranked by wattage, CCT and price for every room.
author: Elume Editorial
intro: False ceilings have become standard in Indian homes, offices and shops, and the LED panel is what actually lights them. Unlike a bulb or batten, a recessed panel sits flush in the POP or gypsum ceiling and washes the room in even, glare-controlled light, which is why a typical living room now takes six to ten panels instead of one central fitting. Multiply that count across a flat or an office floor and panel selection becomes a real budget decision. This guide ranks the ten panels worth specifying on Elume in 2026, judged on wattage, colour temperature options, mounting type and per-unit price.

Shortlist on four things. First, wattage by room: 6W panels for passages and accents, 10-12W as the general-purpose workhorse, 15-24W for larger rooms, and 36W 2x2 panels for offices. Second, colour temperature: 3000K warm for bedrooms and lounges, 4000K neutral for kitchens, 6500K daylight for work areas, or a 3-in-1 panel that switches between all three. Third, shape and mounting: round or square, recessed for false ceilings, surface-mounted where there is no cavity. Fourth, the Elume price against MRP, because panel discounts run deep and change the maths on a 40-panel order.

items.name: Nimbus LED Panel SQ (10W, 4000K)
items.body: The Nimbus is the panel we would specify by default across a full false ceiling. At 10W it is the right general-purpose output for Indian room sizes, and the 4000K neutral white is the most forgiving colour temperature in the catalogue: whiter than warm, softer than daylight, and comfortable in living rooms and kitchens alike. At around Rs 436 on Elume against a Rs 850 MRP, filling a room with six panels costs less than many single decorative fittings. A round 3000K version exists if you prefer warm light.

items.name: Trim NXT LED Panel RD V2 (15W, 6500K)
items.body: The Trim NXT V2 is the value surprise of the range: a 15W round panel in 6500K daylight for around Rs 402 on Elume, barely more than most 10W panels and less than half its Rs 865 MRP. That extra output makes it the right pick for kitchens, study corners and work areas where you want crisp, bright light rather than ambience. Mixing these in task zones with softer panels elsewhere is the cleanest way to layer a false ceiling on a budget.
items.bestFor: Bright task lighting at a 10W price

items.name: Octane + Square LED Panel (12W, 3000K)
items.body: The Octane line is Havells' mainstream recessed panel for homes, shops and showrooms, and this 12W square version in 3000K warm white is its sweet spot. Warm light at proper output is what most Indian living rooms and bedrooms actually want in the evening, and the square cutout suits the grid-like layouts most false-ceiling designers draw. At around Rs 662 on Elume against a Rs 1,130 MRP, it prices comfortably between the budget Trim panels and the premium surface ranges.

items.name: Fazer Neo 3-in-1 LED Panel SQ (10W)
items.body: The Fazer Neo 3-in-1 solves the colour temperature argument by switching between warm, neutral and daylight from the wall switch, so one panel model serves the whole house and the client can change their mind after handover. At 10W it covers general-purpose duty, and at around Rs 362 on Elume it costs about the same as fixed-colour panels, which makes standardising on it an easy call for contractors who want one SKU in the van instead of three.
items.bestFor: One panel SKU, three light colours

items.name: Octane LED Panel Round (18W, 6500K)
items.body: When a 10-12W panel leaves a large room dim, the 18W Octane round is the next step up without jumping to commercial fittings. In 6500K daylight it suits big kitchens, dining areas, offices and shop floors where brightness sells the space. Fewer high-output panels can also beat many small ones: cleaner ceiling, fewer cutouts, less wiring. At around Rs 911 on Elume against a Rs 1,559 MRP, price the room both ways before finalising the layout.
items.bestFor: Large rooms needing fewer, brighter panels

items.name: Trim NXT LED Panel SQ V2 (20W, 6500K)
items.body: The 20W square Trim NXT V2 is the strongest recessed output on this list and still lands under Rs 750: around Rs 725 on Elume versus a Rs 1,255 MRP. Use it where a false ceiling has to do serious work, in deep kitchens, home offices, clinics and reception areas lit entirely from panels with no supporting fittings. Spec-sheet buyers should note the wattage per rupee here is the best in the Havells recessed range this year.

items.name: Trim Plus LED Panel Round (6W, 4000K)
items.body: Every false ceiling needs small panels too: passages, wardrobes, toilets, balconies and the accent ring around a ceiling cove. The 6W Trim Plus in 4000K neutral is the right tool for those spots, bright enough to be useful and dim enough not to glare in a narrow corridor. At around Rs 338 on Elume it is close to bulb pricing, so there is no reason to leave transition spaces on old fittings when the rest of the home moves to panels.

items.name: Octane Plus 2X2 LED Panel (36W, 3000K)
items.body: The 2x2 ft format is the office standard, dropping straight into grid ceilings and replacing the old 4-tube fluorescent fixture with a single 36W LED sheet of even light. Havells pitches this Octane Plus at offices, shops and showrooms, and at around Rs 1,764 on Elume against a Rs 2,700 MRP it undercuts most commercial lighting quotes. The 3000K warm version stocked here suits showrooms and hospitality; check layout spacing so panels land on the grid, not the tiles.

items.name: Trim Cosmo LED Surface Panel RD (24W, 3000K)
items.body: Not every ceiling has a cavity to recess into. The Trim Cosmo is a surface-mounted panel that fixes directly to a plain concrete or wooden ceiling, bringing panel-style even light to rooms without false ceilings. At 24W in 3000K warm white it can carry a bedroom or mid-size living room on its own. Around Rs 975 on Elume against a steep Rs 2,335 MRP makes it one of the deeper discounts in the lighting catalogue right now.

items.name: Aurika Surface LED Panel 30W CCT
items.body: The Aurika is the premium pick: a 30W surface panel with CCT control, meaning the colour temperature itself is adjustable rather than fixed at purchase. That flexibility suits master bedrooms and living rooms that shift from bright daytime use to warm evening light. At around Rs 4,017 on Elume it costs as much as ten budget panels, so treat it as a feature fitting for one or two key rooms, not a whole-house specification. A 60W music version exists for home theatres.

buyingTips: Count panels by coverage, not by habit: as a working rule one 10-12W panel covers roughly the area a small room corner needs, and a standard bedroom takes four to six panels. Sketch the ceiling grid first, then order, so you buy the layout rather than a guess.

Zone your colour temperatures: 3000K warm in bedrooms and lounges, 4000K neutral in kitchens and passages, 6500K daylight over task areas. Or standardise on 3-in-1 panels like the Fazer Neo and decide room by room after installation.

Confirm recessed versus surface before ordering: recessed panels like the Nimbus and Octane need a false-ceiling cavity and the right cutout size, while plain ceilings need surface models like the Trim Cosmo or Aurika. Mixing them on one order is normal.

Buy 10-15 percent extra panels in the same batch. Matching the exact light colour of a panel bought a year later is difficult, and a few spares from the original batch keep the ceiling uniform when a unit eventually needs replacing.
For offices, compare one 36W 2x2 Octane Plus panel against four small panels per bay: the 2x2 usually wins on cost, wiring time and evenness of light in a grid ceiling.

faq.q: How many LED panels do I need for one room?
faq.a: It depends on room size, panel wattage and how bright you want the space. As a practical starting point, a standard bedroom is comfortable with four to six 10-12W panels, while larger living rooms use six to ten or step up to 15-20W panels. Sketch a symmetric grid on the ceiling plan first and let the layout set the count.

faq.q: What is the difference between recessed and surface panels?
faq.a: Recessed panels drop into a cutout in a false ceiling so only the trim and light are visible; they need a POP or gypsum cavity behind them. Surface panels mount directly onto any flat ceiling with no cavity required. If your ceiling is plain concrete, choose surface models like the Havells Trim Cosmo or Aurika.

faq.q: Which colour temperature should I pick: 3000K, 4000K or 6500K?
faq.a: 3000K is warm and cosy, best for bedrooms and lounges. 6500K is crisp daylight for kitchens, studies and work areas. 4000K sits in between and is the safest single choice for a whole home. If you cannot decide, 3-in-1 panels like the Havells Fazer Neo let you switch between all three from the wall switch.

faq.q: Can LED panels be repaired, or do they need full replacement?
faq.a: Most panels are sealed units, so a failure usually means replacing the panel or its driver rather than repairing the LEDs. This is why buying branded panels with warranties, keeping a few spares from the same batch, and using a common size across the house are the three habits that make long-term maintenance painless.

faq.q: Are LED panels expensive to run compared to tubelights?
faq.a: No, they are typically cheaper per unit of light. A room lit by five 10W panels draws 50W in total, similar to a single old fluorescent fitting, while lighting the space far more evenly. Panels also let you switch zones separately, so you rarely run the full ceiling at once, which trims consumption further.
